name: Nld

unions.name: Nld
unions.comment:
An `Nld a` is a parser that produces values of type `a` from a sequence of tokens.
It explores multiple parse branches lazily, preferring matches where tokens appear
in the specified order and closer together.

values.name: andMap
values.comment:
Apply a parser producing a function to a parser producing a value.

This enables pipe-style composition for building parsers with arbitrary arity:

    runList
        (succeed Tuple.pair
            |> andMap (word "buy")
            |> andMap nat
        )
        [ "buy", "3" ]
    --> [ ( "buy", 3 ) ]
values.type: Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld (a -> b) -> Nld.Nld b

values.name: andThen
values.comment: Sequence two parsers. The second parser can depend on the result of the first.
values.type: (a -> Nld.Nld b) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b

values.name: autocomplete
values.comment:
Get autocomplete suggestions as a Peach.

This walks the parse tree, collecting suggestions at every point where
the parser fails due to missing tokens. The result is a lazy stream
of suggestion sets, ordered by parse weight.

values.name: boolean
values.comment:
Match a boolean token. Accepts case-insensitive variations:
"true", "True", "TRUE", "false", "False", "FALSE", etc.

    runList boolean [ "true" ]
    --> [ True ]

    runList boolean [ "set", "verbose", "True" ]
    --> [ True ]

values.name: choice
values.comment: Try multiple parsers and return all successful parses.

values.name: combine
values.comment:
Run each parser in the list and collect the results into a list.

    runList (combine [ word "a", word "b" ]) [ "a", "b" ]
    --> [ [ "a", "b" ] ]

Individual elements can use `words` for synonyms:

    runList (combine [ words [ "remove", "delete" ], words [ "file", "document" ] ]) [ "please", "erase", "the", "document" ]
    --> []

values.name: float
values.comment:
Match a floating-point number.

    runList float [ "3.14" ]
    --> [ 3.14 ]

    runList float [ "set", "rate", "to", "2.5" ]
    --> [ 2.5 ]

values.name: grammar
values.comment:
Extract the `Grammar` from an `Nld` parser. The `Grammar` is a first-class
description of what the parser expects, useful for introspection and serialization.

    import Nld.Grammar exposing (Grammar(..))
    import Nld.TokenType exposing (TokenType(..))

    grammar (word "hello")
    --> Literal "hello"

    grammar (tuple2 (word "buy") nat)
    --> Seq [ Literal "buy", Token NatToken ]

values.name: indexedBoolean
values.comment:
Match a boolean token and return it with its position.
Accepts case-insensitive variations: "true", "True", "TRUE", "false", "False", "FALSE", etc.

values.name: indexedFloat
values.comment: Match a floating-point number and return it with its position.

values.name: indexedInt
values.comment: Match an integer (positive or negative) and return it with its position.

values.name: indexedMinimalToken
values.comment:
Match any of several weighted tokens, returning both the token and its position.
Lower weights are preferred.
values.type: List.List ( Basics.Float, String.String ) -> Nld.Nld ( String.String, Basics.Int )

values.name: indexedNat
values.comment: Match a natural number and return it with its position.

values.name: indexedToken
values.comment: Match any token and return it with its position.

values.name: indexedTokenMatching
values.comment: Match tokens satisfying a predicate and return with position.

values.name: indexedTokenOfType
values.comment:
Match a token of a given `TokenType` using a custom parse function.
The `TokenType` is used in the `Grammar` representation.

This is used to define `indexedNat`, `indexedFloat`, etc.
values.type: Nld.TokenType.TokenType -> (String.String -> Maybe.Maybe a) -> Nld.Nld ( a, Basics.Int )

values.name: indexedWord
values.comment:
Match a specific word and return both the word and its position.

    runList (indexedWord "cat") [ "the", "cat", "sat" ]
    --> [ ( "cat", 1 ) ]

values.name: indexedWords
values.comment:
Match any of the given words and return the canonical (first) word with its position.

Note: Like `words`, this canonicalizes the result to the first word in the list.
For example, `indexedWords ["hello", "hi"]` with input `["hi"]` returns `[("hello", 0)]`.

values.name: int
values.comment:
Match an integer (positive or negative).

    runList (tuple2 (word "offset") int) [ "offset", "-5", "pixels" ]
    --> [ ( "offset", -5 ) ]

values.name: map
values.comment:
Transform the result of a parser.

    runList (map String.toUpper (word "hello")) [ "hello" ]
    --> [ "HELLO" ]

values.name: map2
values.comment: Combine two parsers.
values.type: (a -> b -> c) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c

values.name: map3
values.comment: Combine three parsers.
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c -> Nld.Nld d

values.name: map4
values.comment: Combine four parsers.
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> e) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c -> Nld.Nld d -> Nld.Nld e

values.name: map5
values.comment: Combine five parsers.
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c -> Nld.Nld d -> Nld.Nld e -> Nld.Nld f

values.name: map6
values.comment: Combine six parsers.
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c -> Nld.Nld d -> Nld.Nld e -> Nld.Nld f -> Nld.Nld g

values.name: map7
values.comment: Combine seven parsers.
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> h) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c -> Nld.Nld d -> Nld.Nld e -> Nld.Nld f -> Nld.Nld g -> Nld.Nld h

values.name: map8
values.comment: Combine eight parsers.
values.type: (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> h -> i) -> Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c -> Nld.Nld d -> Nld.Nld e -> Nld.Nld f -> Nld.Nld g -> Nld.Nld h -> Nld.Nld i

values.name: minimalToken
values.comment:
Match any of several weighted tokens. Lower weights are preferred.

    runTake 1 (minimalToken [ ( 0.0, "delete" ), ( 1.0, "remove" ) ]) [ "remove", "delete" ]
    --> [ "delete" ]

values.name: nat
values.comment:
Match a natural number (non-negative integer).

    runList (tuple2 (word "buy") nat) [ "buy", "3", "apples" ]
    --> [ ( "buy", 3 ) ]

values.name: phrase
values.comment:
Match all of the words in the list (using `word` for each) and return them
joined by spaces.

    runList (phrase [ "delete", "file" ]) [ "please", "delete", "the", "file" ]
    --> [ "delete file" ]

    runList (tuple2 (phrase [ "set", "volume" ]) nat) [ "set", "the", "volume", "to", "11" ]
    --> [ ( "set volume", 11 ) ]

values.name: repeat
values.comment:
Match zero or more occurrences of a parser.

Returns all prefixes of the greedy (in-order) match:

  - For `repeat nat` with `["1", "2", "3"]`, returns:
    `[[1, 2, 3], [1, 2], [1], []]`

The greedy collection always takes elements in position order.

values.name: run
values.comment: Run a parser on TokenPositions and return all results.
values.type: Nld.Nld a -> Nld.TokenPositions -> List.List ( Basics.Float, a )

values.name: runList
values.comment:
Run a parser on a list of tokens and return all results.
Results are ordered by priority (best matches first).

    runList (word "hello") [ "hello", "world" ]
    --> [ "hello" ]

    runList (tuple2 (word "delete") (word "file")) [ "file", "delete" ]
    --> [ ( "delete", "file" ) ]
values.type: Nld.Nld a -> List.List String.String -> List.List a

values.name: runTake
values.comment:
Run a parser and take only the first n results.

    runTake 1 (word "cat") [ "the", "cat", "sat" ]
    --> [ "cat" ]
values.type: Basics.Int -> Nld.Nld a -> List.List String.String -> List.List a

values.name: succeed
values.comment:
A parser that always succeeds with the given value without consuming any tokens.

This is the fundamental building block for applicative-style parsing with `andMap`.

    runList (succeed 42) [ "any", "tokens" ]
    --> [ 42 ]

values.name: token
values.comment:
Match any token and return it.

    runTake 1 token [ "anything" ]
    --> [ "anything" ]

values.name: tokenMatching
values.comment:
Match tokens satisfying a predicate.

    runList (tokenMatching (String.endsWith ".txt")) [ "open", "report.txt" ]
    --> [ "report.txt" ]

values.name: tokenize
values.comment:
Split a text string into tokens suitable for use with `runList`, `runTake`,
and other `Nld` runners. It handles:

  - Whitespace: splits on whitespace, discarding it.
  - camelCase: splits at lowercase-to-uppercase boundaries.
  - Uppercase runs: keeps runs of uppercase letters together, splitting before the
    final uppercase letter when followed by lowercase (e.g. "HTML" and "Parser").
  - Alpha/numeric boundaries: splits between letters and digits.
  - Punctuation: each non-alphanumeric, non-whitespace character becomes its own token.

Examples:

    tokenize "hello world"
    --> [ "hello", "world" ]

    tokenize "camelCase"
    --> [ "camel", "Case" ]

    tokenize "HTMLParser"
    --> [ "HTML", "Parser" ]

    tokenize "abcra123"
    --> [ "abcra", "123" ]

    tokenize "(hi!)"
    --> [ "(", "hi", "!", ")" ]

    tokenize "setHTMLParser"
    --> [ "set", "HTML", "Parser" ]

values.name: topK
values.comment:
Get autocomplete suggestions for incomplete input.
Returns a list of sets of tokens that could complete the parse.

    import Set

    topK 5 (word "buy") []
    -- Returns [ Set.fromList [ "buy" ] ]

    topK 5 (tuple2 (word "buy") (word "apples")) [ "buy" ]
    -- Returns [ Set.fromList [ "apples" ] ]

This explores the parse tree lazily, finding all points where the parser
needs tokens that aren't present in the input.
values.type: Basics.Int -> Nld.Nld a -> List.List String.String -> List.List (Set.Set String.String)

values.name: tuple2
values.comment:
Combine two parsers into a tuple.

    runList (tuple2 (word "delete") (word "file")) [ "delete", "file" ]
    --> [ ( "delete", "file" ) ]
values.type: Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld ( a, b )

values.name: tuple3
values.comment: Combine three parsers into a tuple.
values.type: Nld.Nld a -> Nld.Nld b -> Nld.Nld c -> Nld.Nld ( a, b, c )

values.name: wanted
values.comment:
Returns the set of tokens that the parser is looking for.
This is used internally for optimization but can be useful for debugging.

    import Set

    wanted (word "hello")
    --> Set.fromList [ "hello" ]

values.name: word
values.comment:
Match a specific word/token.

    runList (word "delete") [ "please", "delete", "this" ]
    --> [ "delete" ]

values.name: words
values.comment:
Match any of the given words, preferring earlier ones in the list.
Useful for synonyms.

    runList (words [ "delete", "remove", "erase" ]) [ "please", "erase", "this" ]
    --> [ "delete" ]

The result is canonicalized to the first word in the list.

unions.name: Grammar
unions.comment: A first-class representation of an NLD-based grammar.

values.name: choice
values.comment:
Combine a list of `Grammar` values into a single `Choice`, flattening
any nested `Choice` nodes.

    import Nld.TokenType exposing (TokenType(..))

    choice [ Literal "yes", Literal "no" ]
    --> Choice [ Literal "yes", Literal "no" ]

    choice [ Literal "a", Choice [ Literal "b", Literal "c" ] ]
    --> Choice [ Literal "a", Literal "b", Literal "c" ]

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
Decode a `Grammar` from JSON.

    import Json.Decode as Decode

    Decode.decodeString decoder """{"tag":"anyToken"}"""
    --> Ok AnyToken

values.name: encode
values.comment:
Encode a `Grammar` as a JSON value.

    import Json.Encode as Encode
    import Nld.TokenType exposing (TokenType(..))

    encode (Token NatToken)
    --> Encode.object [ ( "tag", Encode.string "token" ), ( "value", Encode.object [ ( "tag", Encode.string "nat" ) ] ) ]

values.name: seq
values.comment:
Combine a list of `Grammar` values into a single `Seq`, flattening
any nested `Seq` nodes.

    import Nld.TokenType exposing (TokenType(..))

    seq [ Literal "hello", Literal "world" ]
    --> Seq [ Literal "hello", Literal "world" ]

    seq [ Literal "a", Seq [ Literal "b", Literal "c" ] ]
    --> Seq [ Literal "a", Literal "b", Literal "c" ]

unions.name: TokenType
unions.comment:
The type of a token appearing in a `Grammar`. An autocomplete provider can
use this information to provide a richer experience when editing text that is
meant to be parsed by that grammar.

Note: the `String` in `Currency` is an ISO 4217 currency code.

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
Decode a `TokenType` from JSON.

    import Json.Decode as Decode

    Decode.decodeString decoder "{\"tag\":\"nat\"}"
    --> Ok NatToken

values.name: encode
values.comment:
Encode a `TokenType` to JSON.

    import Json.Encode as Encode

    Encode.encode 0 (encode NatToken)
    --> "{\"tag\":\"nat\"}"

name: Peach

unions.name: Peach
unions.comment:
A `Peach a` represents a lazy priority search computation that can yield
multiple weighted results. Results with smaller weights are explored first.

Internally, this is represented as a priority queue of either:

  - Concrete weighted values
  - Thunks that produce more `Peach` elements when forced

This allows lazy evaluation: results are only computed when extracted.

values.name: choose
values.comment:
Choose between multiple `Peach` computations, exploring them in priority order.

    choose
        [ peach [ ( 1.0, "a" ) ]
        , peach [ ( 2.0, "b" ) ]
        , peach [ ( 0.5, "c" ) ]
        ]
        |> toList
    --> [ ( 0.5, "c" ), ( 1.0, "a" ), ( 2.0, "b" ) ]

values.name: each
values.comment:
Create a `Peach` from a list, treating each element equally (weight 0).

    each [ "a", "b", "c" ]
        |> toList
        |> List.length
    --> 3

values.name: fail
values.comment:
A `Peach` that yields no results (represents failure).

    fail
        |> head
    --> Nothing

values.name: flatMap
values.comment:
Chain computations where the second depends on the result of the first.
The weights are combined additively.

    peach [ ( 1.0, "a" ), ( 2.0, "b" ) ]
        |> flatMap (\x -> peach [ ( 0.5, x ++ "1" ), ( 1.0, x ++ "2" ) ])
        |> take 4
    --> [ ( 1.5, "a1" ), ( 2.0, "a2" ), ( 2.5, "b1" ), ( 3.0, "b2" ) ]

**Laziness:** `flatMap` is lazy - it doesn't immediately evaluate all branches.
Instead, it creates thunks that are evaluated on-demand as results are extracted.
values.type: (a -> Peach.Peach b) -> Peach.Peach a -> Peach.Peach b

values.name: head
values.comment:
Get the first (lowest weight) result from a `Peach` computation, if any.

This forces evaluation of thunks as needed to find the minimum-weight value.

    peach [ ( 2.0, "b" ), ( 1.0, "a" ) ]
        |> head
    --> Just ( 1.0, "a" )

    fail
        |> head
    --> Nothing

values.name: lazy
values.comment:
Create a lazy `Peach` from a thunk. The thunk will only be evaluated
when results are extracted. This is the key primitive for lazy evaluation.

    lazy 0.0 (\() -> peach [ ( 1.0, "computed" ) ])
        |> head
    --> Just ( 1.0, "computed" )

The first argument is the minimum possible weight of results from this thunk.
This is used for priority ordering - thunks with lower weights are forced first.

values.name: map
values.comment:
Transform the values in a `Peach` computation.

    peach [ ( 1.0, 2 ), ( 2.0, 3 ) ]
        |> map (\x -> x * 2)
        |> toList
    --> [ ( 1.0, 4 ), ( 2.0, 6 ) ]

Note: `map` is lazy - it wraps transformations in thunks for deferred items.

values.name: optional
values.comment:
Convert an `Optional` value to a `Peach`.

    optional (Just "hello")
        |> head
    --> Just ( 0.0, "hello" )

    optional Nothing
        |> head
    --> Nothing

values.name: peach
values.comment:
Create a `Peach` from a list of weighted values. The values will be explored
in order of increasing weight (smallest first).

    peach [ ( 2.0, "b" ), ( 1.0, "a" ), ( 3.0, "c" ) ]
        |> head
    --> Just ( 1.0, "a" )

values.name: rankBy
values.comment:
Rank values by a function and create a `Peach` from them.

    rankBy (\s -> toFloat (String.length s)) [ "apple", "kiwi", "banana" ]
        |> head
    --> Just ( 4.0, "kiwi" )

values.name: succeed
values.comment:
A `Peach` that yields exactly one result with weight 0.

    succeed "hello"
        |> head
    --> Just ( 0.0, "hello" )

values.name: take
values.comment:
Take the first `n` results from a `Peach` computation, ordered by weight.

This is the primary way to extract results lazily. Only the minimum number
of thunks needed to produce `n` results will be evaluated.

    peach [ ( 2.0, "b" ), ( 1.0, "a" ), ( 3.0, "c" ) ]
        |> take 2
    --> [ ( 1.0, "a" ), ( 2.0, "b" ) ]
values.type: Basics.Int -> Peach.Peach a -> List.List ( Basics.Float, a )

values.name: toList
values.comment:
Convert a `Peach` computation to a list of all results, ordered by weight (smallest first).

**Warning:** This forces evaluation of all thunks. For infinite or very large
search spaces, use `take` instead.

    peach [ ( 2.0, "b" ), ( 1.0, "a" ), ( 3.0, "c" ) ]
        |> toList
    --> [ ( 1.0, "a" ), ( 2.0, "b" ), ( 3.0, "c" ) ]
